[[File:Sweetrip.png|center|link=|450px]]






This place is chickenshit, honey.




Hey, don’t say that! Look down, Brandon—I’m eating chicken sticks.





So what? It’s not that hard to tell your brain to shut itself down and stop thinking about actual chicken shit.





Well, just look around. It’s... beautiful. The green of the grass, the blue of the blooming sky. Definitely not chickenshit, let me tell you. Nothing’s brown and dirty here.





The only thing keeping us from brown and dirty is this blanket. The soil’s wet. And the grass? It’s stabbing me in the ass.





Don’t be pessimistic, Brandon. Remember what we used to say?





You are my Tree of Elixir. It doesn’t sound as cool as it used to be back in 10th grade, though.





It is cool! If you're about not being cool, go look up the hill. Look up to the sun. You’ve got your sunglasses on, too.









Sigh. I’m still pissed off about yesterday’s presentation.





Doesn’t matter when we do it. I think this can still be a cherished moment.





The fanciest thing we’ve got here is an In-N-Out burger.








I think we should do this again some time.








Whatever.




You don’t look too happy about it.




I don’t.








Brandon, I know you're happy inside. Especially when I'm here. Just look at your funny face.





I'm thinking about it.

'''Level LX presents itself as a dipteral ancient Greek temple. The ''naos'', the inner sanctuary where sculptures of deities are traditionally placed, is considerably smaller compared to the surrounding peristyle. Its columns stretch upward endlessly, vanishing into a ceiling of white fog hanging several hundred meters above, obscuring the ''entablature''. False brick walls as thick as dams hamper access to the naos. In addition, misleading ''pronaoi'', vestibules that lead to the sanctuary, scatter the complex. Some of these lead to unknown locations and are usually marked by the absence of grilles and a profound darkness lurking behind the opening.'''

'''What lies beyond is enveloped in the same persistent white fog. An invisible wall bars access to what would be the crepidoma; the ramp, by extension, remains obscured. Ionic order columns are arranged in a hexagonal lattice, their placement maximizing count and minimizing visibility. The intercolumniation is fixed at II.LXXIV meters, forming precise LX-degree angles—possibly echoing the temple’s hypothesized triangular pediment. This perimeter is where most wanderers remain, often indefinitely. It is also where most corpses are found, decomposing near the base of the columns. See Detail Fig. VIIb, compiled in MCMXXIII from an unknown source, for further reference. Though never officially measured, the columns are estimated to rise approximately MLXXXIII feet, with a diameter of IX.LXXXIV feet. Their square marble bases are perfectly uniform, their geometry bordering on divine, or on something far older than divinity. The columns themselves contain a suspiciously excessive volume of concrete. Several extraction groups have attempted to mine them, targeting the bases for their valuable composition. Some believe the II.LXXIV-meter spacing represents a compromise, though it remains unclear between whom or between what or even between when. The level’s misleading pronaoi features two front columns, seemingly symmetrical, but concealing two grille-lined gaps that descend into voids. Apart from these and the naos, this is the only elevation shift present in the level: a stylobate, III meters tall, that wanderers must ascend.'''

It smells like metallic, rotten meat.'''

'''There is nothing in the fullness of light. Do not panic when escaping. Hold your breath, take a few long steps, and exhale—slowly. The peristyle is where one must remain. Bypassing the wall is not advisable. If one reaches into it, the length of an arm—perhaps more—may be lost. Falling off is, in other words, suicide: the chair and rope of this level. The similarity lies not in method, but in the power of the made-weak and helpless, and the quiet spite of the act. Level LX appears to reside within [[The Broken|The Broken]], though it exhibits none of its known anomalies. Despite its brightness, it does not blind, not even when standing directly before the invisible wall. There is order in the whiteness. No [[Object 32|Corruptionite]] have been witnessed. How the ever-present light remains visible to the naked eye is unknown. But, if one stands flush against the wall and stares for hours, a mirrored silhouette of the level slowly emerges. Half-formed, beyond the white. Some report having "accessed" these areas. Most sources, however, attribute them to visual hallucinations. Many from different sources. Sources from wanderers of differing backgrounds. Their nations were different. Nations. Visual. VISUAL NATIONS! VISTA-NATIONS. INTO VALEN-ATION. VALIDATION—VAL, VAL, VALENTINE. W/W/W/. VALE:''' '''''Depression''''' '''''of the''''' '''''hills,''''' '''''depression''''' '''''of the''''' '''''hills.''''' There They Saw Hestia

Of what chance a bottled water would spawn out of nowhere in here? oh a Special Grateful Thank You, Tree of Elixir I feel warmth under your roots You are my Valerie I will take everything from you of course, darling— See? it ain’t that much to ask I may have cursed and insulted you But I have begged, begged all my will and my knees already started to hurt and not my tongue it was simply an illusion.





[[File:BOO ITS BLOOD SCARY.gif|link=|center]]




Grabbed it with my hand All the strength I had I put into that grip—onto the Tree of Elixir, into the bottle It was hard plastic but I could feel it giving way, like it was on the verge of disintegrating into dust, into microplastics, into nothing.

The taste of almond hit my tongue. It had never felt more refreshing. It was like crushing a coconut’s mesocarp with your bare hands, feeling that coolness flood out, a sudden calm And ''oh'' was it holy The Spirit was circling me or maybe it was just wind or maybe memory I could see beaches—warm and wide—Israel maybe, or some ancient dream of Israel, blurred with palm trees, the warmth of sand, and somewhere in the distance, Greek columns leaning gently in the sun All of that, packed into this cloudy white water. It was wonderful.



































I believe I can form thoughts now. I may need some time to adapt to my condition now. My body is a classroom, and the cells are students. They take instructions from the teacher—my brain, or sometimes from each other—and day by day, they learn to do better. Never felt like a factory to me, to be honest. Factories don’t teach; they extract. You work, break, and earn just enough to afford that apartment, but not enough to repair it or get health insurance. The sun’s warmth had melted the classroom’s cool sterility, just like Valerie said it would, years ago. I didn’t walk too far. I only passed by a few columns... but it did felt like an eternity. Yet, I couldn’t recall a moment where I could sense the dissolved salt in my sweat creeping down my forehead. It was there. And the next second passed... Gone. Almond Water is the Tree of Elixir when it comes to the mind only. You know, whatever. At least it killed a bit of time. I could feel a wall swoop behind me. The columns narrowed in the distance. Not dramatically, but just enough to feel it. It looked like the painfully slow growth of a tree, and the equally aching nostalgia that comes with seeing something so familiar begins to change. A tourist might pass without noticing, but someone like Methuselah would: the old man walks by it daily, peeling bark for his hearth, and knows every crevice by heart. And I am, in every sense, Methuselah— “No, you’re not, silly.” A female voice drifted in from behind me. The water in my stomach jolted. My whole body recoiled. I could feel my throat tighten, my insides twisting. The legs, my roots. The torso, my trunk. The liquid I had so carefully kept down was boiling now, viscera threatening to rip apart. “You look like you’ve just seen Satan with that mouth of yours,” she said, almost playfully. “Maybe you ''are'' Methuselah. Look at your face!” The problem, however, was that I hadn’t turned around and seen her face-to-face yet.





'''In the absence of a bona fide pronaoi, subjects may encounter a █████████████ instance. As per all reports, these manifestations consistently display the following traits:''' * '''Gender presentation tailored to subject’s attraction.''' * '''Self-given name derived from Greco-Roman deity.''' * '''Simulated affectionate behavior, typically mirroring a love—'''





I braced myself to see what kind of person she was. The featureless black hoodie and rotten chicken smell don’t say much if you’re in the Backrooms. Faces don’t either, but expressions do. The little eyebrow hairs—do they flop or stand up? Are those lips bright red from lipstick, split lips, or maybe... blood from razor cuts? “You’re a pussy.” Jolted awake. “I’m sorry?” I snapped. Then I finally looked back. “If I were a [[Entity 10|Skin-Stealer]], you’d be as good as skin soup boiling in a hearth by now.” She promptly turned away from me, presumably to walk into a column and crash. Her expression suggested annoyance more than the standard, thank-God-there’s-another-human-being expression. Of all the things possible in this place beyond walking and trying to climb the columns—and fail, of course—the only conclusion I could think of was that she hadn’t drank water, maybe even longer than I did. Those brief seconds I saw her face were more than enough. She was eccentric, like she was born in the Backrooms. Looking at her, circling around a column. Looking at the shining light from the invisible sky outside. White, yes, it was a perfect white, but not bright at all. She still, however, blocked her face with her hand for shade. Must be sensitive eyes. “You’re not gonna ask me for supplies, are you?” I thought she might need a drop of my bottle or two. She kept her hand in place and looked at me. “Not yet. I’m busy doing something.” “Is it some kind of ritual?” “Yeah. Anything for good exercise.”





[[File:Vestahiesta.png|350px|center|link=]]




“I think I’ve seen you before,” I asked. “Where?” “It’s something about you. I recognize your...” Hesitation. What word should I use? Perhaps, “...groove.” “‘Groove?’ Are you a Beatles fan?” “I’m not that old.” Wiped my face and turned away in case she lunged at me. “But, no offense, you do—” “I don’t mind,” she replied just before I could finish my last word. “Sure, you don’t.” I still had to be cautious, so I took a step forward and merely twisted my head. She pulled off her hoodie, and underneath was... a rotten linen handkerchief? I squinted. Was it just covering her stomach—like she was hiding self-cannibalizing marks—or was that her actual torso? Either way, I was about to throw hands— “Don’t make up your mind yet, Brad. I’ve got more clothes under this.” Brad? What did she mean by that? I blurted, “Brad—?” “You don’t feel cold around here?” “Um...” I was closer to assassinating this weird leader-of-a-ghostly-organization than her clothes were to each other. Oh, how fast things can change, huh? “Are you the CIA?” I questioned. “What?” she remarked as if she were the one blundering the interrogation. “How’d you know my name?” I loosened my backpack filled with sticky and smelly daggers. “You definitely weren’t muttering to yourself just before.” “You ''are'' the CIA. You were following me the whole time!” “Don’t think I’m the Tree of Elixir. Look at my clothes, Brad.” Her hoodie was on the ground, lying and dirtying the pristine, white marble. Through my peripheral vision, I saw her gaze toward me that said, “This isn’t funny,” so I sighed. And, yes, she looked like a peasant, or perhaps one who was trying to be a deity with those gold linings around her torn cotton shirt. “You do look old,” I said. “What’s with those clothes? Are you a time traveler?” “You’re not funny.” “Well, okay, Mrs. Stalker. Be judgmental all you want.” “You don’t get it. Or maybe it’s just me. Boredom’s deadlier here. You’d do anything.” She turned back and kneeled to pick up the fallen hoodie, then laid it flat where I stood. Visible dust from her treks on Roman roads glittered around her as she moved and tucked her hair behind, like light reflections in rainfall. “Pretend this is a table. Let’s call him the ‘Talking Table.’” “You can do magic?” I asked. “Yes. I can and do read minds, occasionally,” she whispered. “No, no. You don’t. All you do is listen to them, that’s the extent of what you can do.” “I don’t, Brad. Nobody can.” The hoodie slipped away from me as she pulled it closer to her. “This isn’t the time to joke around.” “You sound like Valerie.” I figured she’d know her from my zombified ramblings. She then grabbed her hair, tied it, and she strutted closer to me. “Maybe that’s the point, Brandon. I already know your name. What about your last?” “Forgot. I’ve given up remembering it ever since I got a badge number from [[M.E.G.|M.E.G.]]” “Interesting.” “You know, I should probably know your name too—” As soon as the last syllable left my lips, she immediately answered, “It’s Hestia.” “Oh, Hestia?. Well, that’s quirky name. Fits you... well. What’s this ‘Talking Table’ thing about anyway?” She sat down, legs crossed, before inviting me to do the same. I complied. “Guess it’d be better if we talked properly. I didn’t want to start arguments. Never intended to.” I remembered her behavior before. She avoided me for most of the time. When engaged with my remarks, she did this ritual—actually two of them—and now we’re here. “You got a reason why you’re doing all this for?” “So we don’t start arguments.” “I won’t hurt you, Hestia.” “It’s not that.” “Well, we don’t have anything to do here anyway. Why don’t you tell me what’s been bothering you?” I let go of my backpack on the floor and grabbed the bottle I took before. “Here, you can have a sip.” “No. Not that either. I just don’t want to get reminded of my parents and siblings.” “What did they do?” “I was running to school—” “Running? School?” “Yes, Brad. School. What about it?” She took out her hand to my arm and slightly grazed by my hair. “Brad? Listen to me!” I could feel my limbs phase through the concrete and my body float away.

“Wake up before you chew my fingers, Brad!” “What again, you?” I said, clicking my tongue. “What’s with the chicken wings you were murmuring?” “Um... I was hungry? Probably. How am I supposed to know” Although I opened up about my past experiences to this random young lady, I still felt the columns closing in on me. The walls of white light in the distance, squishing their way in. I looked around. Felt nothing but the tightening of my lungs and breath... and there it came. A slight brush. I felt a touch again, now on the back of my palm. “I might understand my darling Nero’s speeches more than yours. Hell, maybe even Hitler’s.” “Nothing to find charismatic in a veteran Backrooms explorer, ma’am.” “That’s the only thing separating you from them. I merely think this is pointless. It’s only a mishap, and people mess up presentations all the time. Just look at both Nero and Hitler, Brad.” “Hitler blew his brains out, though. Easy escape.” The day of the presentation, the sun shone and said good morning and afternoon to the dog walkers. We were stuck in class. The warmth of the sun barely reached in through the window. The warmth... of the sun. “You look like you need to bathe in a hearth.” “You’re weirdly obsessed with hearths, you know that?” “I guess so. Now follow me.” She stood up, grabbed her hoodie off the ground, and slipped it back on. “Just saw something ‘round here.” As far as I know, a hearth’s just a dancey synonym of fireplace. And if there’s any fire in a temple, it’s either big enough to blow you up or small enough to carry. I don’t trust this lady with my words anymore, let alone my non-burnt skin. She sped up while looking behind me, and I followed. “Are you sure you’re not tripping off meth? Slow it down, jeez.” “Don’t speak that in my pops Lord’s home!” “Jesus Christ, pray for me...” “I said, don’t do that!” The only hope I had was taken away from me. Oh, well. I guess she could compensate— She stopped. “You saw something?” I asked. I blinked. Nothing changed. Blinked again—and this time, her head snapped toward me. Not even a millisecond of delay. “Look to your right.” I did. Two rows of pristine walls framed what looked like a vestibule.
Could this be it? Had we found the pronaoi, the one I obsessed over, spiraled over, dramatized in a thousand meaningless ways with strokes of letters I made up in my head, all this for an uneventful day? The day I obsess so much, the moment I lost my faith—the decision between heaven or hell—was buried underneath metaphors that formed for a thousand years? Or maybe even that time with my parents where they... No. I can’t remember. I simply can’t. Forgive me. The giant concrete bricks repeated, stacked endlessly on top of and beside each other. In the Backrooms, your mind clings to anything it can. And everything, inevitably, repeats.

“Don’t let your memories falter, Brad. Not yet. Pretty sure you haven’t gotten enough Almond Water.” She grabbed my hand and tightened it, before pulling me inside the shadows. We passed between the columns, and there the grille marks await us. I couldn’t feel my feet as I’d just stopped running. “Don’t let your memories falter.”

My spine burned. Fire crawled down my back. The air smelled like ash. My waist sizzled—pork barbecue? Or was that my skin unzipping and revealing hell? I tried breathing through my mouth, but all I tasted was ceramic like I was crawling with my tongue only. My body lay flat on the floor. I pushed myself up, chilled by the cold and hot sand-covered tiles beneath me. The marble tiles gripped my skin as I lifted as my belly caught between freezing light and the illusion of heat. The warmth in the light had been stolen before it could arrive. “Don’t mind me,” Hestia said from behind. The non-existent winds blew over me, and I stood shivering. When I came to rub my hands and place them on my chest to warm them, though, I only felt skin. My jacket was gone and I was wandering in the cold light and columns shirtless. “Hestia, this isn’t funny.” Holding a lighter on one hand, she scuffled with my jacket in the other. “I’m a little cold, that’s all.” “Like I’m not cold, too. You already have that thick hoodie, Hestia.” She shook the hoodie and placed it near her face, smelling it. “Whatever chemical stuff’s happening in this level probably made it smell like rotten meat. People walk, run, and starve here, and their corpses decompose. And I’m not wearing this caveman-smelling clothing... And also, I already have this lighter to warm you up anyway.” She spun it around to provoke me. “I mean, you did put it on the floor yourself. Look, it’s a tale as old as time. If you’re trying to hook me up, I must reject. I don’t want to hurt anyone anymore like I did with Valerie.” She then put on the jacket. “You ever use the shower after your mom and sister?” Every neuron of hate buried in my mind got overwhelmed with an electron of confusion. “Hold on, why are we talking about my family now? What do they have to do with this?” “Dad once told me he feels like a chicken boiling under a hearth whenever he showers right after either me or Mom.” “What exactly are you trying to say here?” Once again, eccentric Hestia strikes. “Oh, how I miss the heat.” “That sob story won’t work,” I said. Then the elements decayed. The oxygen decayed, and the concrete columns cracked. She looked me in the eye. She approached me, walking like a droid, limbs all stiff. Her footsteps echoed. A blurry arc, the color of her skin, white. Dragged along my vision. Smack! I was slapped in the face. “That’ll warm you up.” I wanted to retaliate but I’d remembered I made a promise not to hurt her... but only physically. I snapped. “You took my jacket, and now you slap me for wanting to get it back. I know you’re asking me to have sex. Just admit that. But let me be the devil’s advocate here—” She flinched, almost as if trying to make another slap. My whole body snapped and backed away, only to be blocked by a column. She took another step, and I trailed my feet to the side. But then she rubbed my stinging cheek and lingered on my forehead. “Sorry, Brandon. Those things you thought and said...” “What?” Someone who’s got their senses wouldn’t have said that, I believe. “Those things you said about false prayers.” “What false prayers?” “I’m usually not the violent kind, okay? Understand? You talked too much while you were drunk out there. I had to act.” “Hestia, you don’t know what’s going on, don’t you? You’re doing all this to a mentally ill wanderer who hasn’t showered for days. How would your dad think about that?” She turned around, slowly pulling the jacket. It made a subtle rip. She threw it to the ground and punched a column beside her, straight ahead like a boxer. It happened bullet fast. The aftermath was like that of a gunshot. Some of the concrete crumbled and stained the marble floor. Then came the knuckles. Blood red, as if it had been skinned. “Okay, maybe I’m also mentally ill!” She grabbed the dusty jacket and wiped the blood off with it. “All my life, I’ve been alone. Everyone has ignored me, and all I did was spend time beside the hearth with my dad and he doesn’t even do anything. He goes to bars and returns home flat-out drunk except on Sunday nights. Also, not to mention... He gets into bar fights. I don’t know when the time would come before he’d get shot. Even treats work like it’s the bullet in Russian Roulette.” “Hestia?” “Oh, you know what? Maybe I can be the bullet. The bullet that just so happens to blow my dad’s brains out. You know what? I chose this life. I’d chosen to be alone. But you know, who do I let that out to? Human beings? Absolutely ''not''. I'm better than that. Do I do it to myself? What will my dad think when he opens the door and lying there? On my bed my arms slit? Really? No! I’ve got to do it alone in some way in a place I’m familiar with the most. Here, where nobody knows. It’s all bright but it’s... It’s cold. I put this to myself.”

“Look, why don’t we just go in and forget this ever happened. You wanted to do that earlier, right?” “Go in where? I don’t have anywhere else.” “That.” I pointed to the grille marks above. “We climb that bit of wall and get into that void.” “You really trust a hooker, here? It’s a coin flip between eternal torture—hell—or we get to see some old statues.” “What’s up with you? Hooker? Really?” I realized the name Hestia... I know that name. The word called me back to the past during ancient Greek lessons, Hestia. A Greek god, maybe? She talks more like a New Jerseyan than an Olympian. And then all this back and forth, talking me down and joking around like everything was in her control. Now, as my mind meandered about Valerie while my physicality lay—on a column and nude and helplessly cold—that she wants my help? I said, “Hold on, hooker... What hooker? How’d you know that? Do you know what that even means?” Her eyes indicated her muscle tendons had loosened. Even her retina dropped under her eyelids. “Brandon...” “Yeah, well, let’s ask this random mentally ill guy for sex by stealing a jacket. How godly of you, huh? Are you even Greek? Is ‘Hestia’ what your customers call you?” I took a few steps back while she slowly crouched. “You know more about me than yourself. Are you an agent of some kind? Did this little non compos mentis schizophrenic liberal piss off the wife-beating anarchists you’re working for, huh?” Ache in the head. Seeping through like boiling oil. Oh, God. It’s like she’s in my head, desperately and weakly carrying the lighter to burn off the nerves that are pulling me away from her. “My brain’s hurting and I don’t get you at all. You’re not here to help me, Hestia—” “I already told you I’m be insane! I’m non compos mentis, Brandon. I’m the only one here. And you. I declined your Almond Water and you’re falling from two sob stories about your high school girlfriend. What’s with that? My Zeus, Brandon. You’re not 16 anymore.” She went to pick my jacket up from the floor, but her clothes snapped. “If all my efforts to calm you down, to be a therapist for your sorry ass... to which all of that you wasted and pissed in that bottle... then fine. I’ll leave. I’ll make sure I stay quiet, and you stay quiet. Maybe forever, I don’t know.” “What?” The wind blowing through the columns suffocated them. They were crashing into each other, hampered by columns, and knocked on them to go in separate directions as their brothers. A disturbance came. Hestia approached me, and I flinched again. And instead of slapping me or trying to get empathy, she turned around. The echoes of her walking away sounded like she was running on heels from an active shooter. And it reminded me... The knocks on my teacher’s desk. I was standing in front of the classroom, everyone watching the tenseness in my muscles, and trying to breathe deeply to soften them so I could remember my lines. Hestia’s anger and subsequent footsteps echo throughout the building. I feel my limbs tightening, almost having a spasm as the tendons try to squeeze and fill whatever air pocket was left. Then she looked back at me. My Hestia... My Hestia... My Vesta Nation... Vesta... Valentine... Valerie... Oh, Valerie. Oh how I feel the ground depressing and sinking.

I hear footsteps crashing on aluminum plates. She strutted toward me, her breath stinking in rotten chicken. There, the old memories were back. No more pseudo-divorces or any running away to take a walk to freshen the mind. None. Plain, face-to-face now. I couldn’t handle it much longer, and neither was she. She shook me as if I was overdosing, which I probably was, and dragged me by tying up my hands, then put me next to the wall she was previously climbing. I could feel the cold, sandy marble on my back but the warmth on the side of my arm touching the concrete. All of this to use me as a platform, wasn’t it? “I hear you, Brandon. I know you’re there.” Now the columns exhaled the air they had stored so long, they were finally trees. Like before, Hestia tried to climb the giant step, her legs swinging like twigs. It was funny. Perhaps it was what I needed: a little circus by an incompetent clown. If I went on another round of jacket-squatting with her, I'd probably fall deeper than her into insanity. But I don’t think that’s possible right now. Her breaths pulsed between struggling to take air and inhaling so much air that she collapsed to the ground. A few seconds here and there, a grip following a slip and sigh, rinse and repeat again and again. From my point of view, the fall was half her height. “Maybe if you took my bottle instead of my jacket, you wouldn’t look like a meat puck.” I could feel my backpack’s weight increase from before. “Just shut up and help me, Brad. This was ''your'' idea." She came back down, her step mimicking the rattling of a fallen, metal pipe, and she dragged me to the wall. The bricks surrounding where she climbed were as cold as the darkness, but when I jumped and held onto the ledge, her warmth and the sand on my fingers stung. “How long have you been doing this?” “Wouldn’t be as long if you didn’t go into relapse again. You should see a doctor about your fugue state when we get out.” “And when’s that exactly?” “When you help me get up here.” I raised my hand as far as possible, and my arms touched the heat. My hands, however, were bitten by the cold again, all the fire sucked out of it. “It’s cold there.” “Even colder here.” “This is not a good idea, Hestia.” “Brad. See this? These are grille marks. Means we’re perfectly fine going there.” A slight itch at the back of my skull. She looked at me with her perfect circle of a pupil. Its circumference without the eyelids blocking any of it. Her spying device revealed itself. Those eyes. Too trustworthy and spotless on the outside. “You really trust a couple of barbecue racks over a life-or-death situation?” I stepped back, though only a couple of steps. “No, not even that. They decide whether you get bliss or torture for the rest of your existence. Forever. Now, I may have made bad choices in life, and while I still do, just look at me here. But, coming from me, you should know. This is far beyond a bad choice.” “Oh, Brad.” “You have trust issues, Hestia. A lack thereof, that is. Do you go blindly with the crowd? Are there people around here other than us? You are nothing but a scavenger. How about you just stop? I know you’re supposed to turn me over to, what, the cops? That’s what the audience wants, right? Ever since—” “You were the one asking for help, Brad.” “No, I know you came to show me that stupid exercise of yours. And then once we yak around while doing nothing, I keep falling asleep and hitting my head on the corners. Every time you hold me in your arms, I wake up holding all these memories again. What’s up with that? I don’t even know how long we’re here. A few hours, days? You never know. Years? Maybe it’s been centuries here, and you’ve just arrived here with some alien [[M.E.G.|M.E.G.]] tech—” She approached me. Microphones on, I assumed. But all she did was stand stiff, unmoving as if her body were tuned to catch the faintest sound. A gasp of struggle? A breath of airy desperation? Please, come on. Do confessions! That’d be awesome, Brandon! Do one of lust, not love. Even an “I love you” is enough, or an iloveyou.txt sent over the gap between us that might as well be a map to Hyperborea. “Then why don’t you just... kill yourself?” The distance tightened like a knot. “I mean it, Brandon. I’m serious. Do a mercy killing. Whatever god you believe will forgive you. From my past experiences—” She interrupted herself and cleared her words, “I’m sorry, Brandon. I just can’t think clearly now. Brandon. If I were your god, I would understand you.” I searched around the room, muscles jolting, guided by dead eyes. What was I looking for? I would go for a knot this time. Hestia gave the idea. Maybe a knot and a chair could go well. Maybe it’d fit Hestia better than me. “Thanks for the shortcut, therapist!” “Brandon. Let me ask you. Do you act like this when meeting people? Humans are social creatures. If it drives me to say to hang yourself over what you’ve done when seeing another human being, then you’re not human. What even are you? All this over climbing some walls. Fuck you, Brandon. Go sit in a corner and I’ll get ''myself'' out of here.”





I see the words and hear the mouth she said to get out of here, get out of the grass. She says the words herself, one last chance to catch the colors of each letter stroke with their own tones and melodies with my eyes. The grass blades cut the little hair on my legs, the black bush protecting my flesh from Mother Nature, but she didn’t have thick ones, but thin ones, so she was pierced and uncomfortable. Despite that, I was the one complaining. Weeping like a child about the presentation, running around with my mouth while my toes rubbed against the sandpaper-textured picnic blanket and formed deep calluses. I was a tough one. Tough on the outside and inside, like the blanket, but thinly made. Valerie could see through me as I swayed in the wind, and the sewn fabric that made me have holes between them made me translucent. I am getting ahead of myself. I am running to get help, but my soul runs faster, faster than help could throw me to the ground and handcuff me. There’s no help without handcuffs. You don’t tell a murderer to stop killing people. You don’t vent to Hitler or Nero about your loudmouthed friend being hanged by their snapped necks. Oh, but I could see his windpipe stick out! No. You either put them in handcuffs or, if you want to become just like them, blow their brains out—either/or. Now for the things I’d done, the botched presentation and whining-like-a-bitch with Valerie, perhaps this is my handcuff. Eternal torture. Does it seem fair? Why does God allow innocent children to suffer from pointless diseases, or billions of people to die in wars? Isn’t he all-powerful? And so I will exercise free will, the one thing He had given me and not other living creatures and I can do whatever I want without the universe stopping me, let it be pointless, let everything be pointless. Tingling from behind my head like fingernails were digging dip into my skull. What’s that? Ignore it, ignore it, Brandon, don’t give in That’s the universe trying to convince you it is not real Nothing matters when all you could choose is between eternal torture or the end of everything you knew and cared for and touched and caressed every person you loved and hated or both Every animal you loved Every food you liked That In-N-Out Burger with Valerie Everything. I wasn’t here because it was all written I was here because I was a thin blanket and let the whirlwind shoot me out of the grass fields from Valerie and let the rest of the storms carry me where I am now, that is not the universe deciding where to go to But that is what random atoms and energy wanted me to They’re matter and they don’t matter once their action has passed They get absorbed into stars and consumed by black holes and gone like me But I am alive Where I went is outside of the universe’s jurisdiction because I am outside of it I am decided by random, vulnerable, code. I decide if even an omniscient cannot determine where I go then this random event may be the best for me and my free will and the light in the black hole wasn’t exactly a star but it shone brighter.

“Hey,” she said from afar. She wasn’t shouting, but however normal-sounding the tone was, it didn’t matter. The pillars still echoed it. I now stood on a hard floor, no longer on soft, vulnerable code. My position will remain until the last black hole emits its last breath. My leather boots will finally have loosened their laces when the last photon breaks out of its shell. You hear me, Hestia? I will continue walking into the dark, white light, and bathe in it forever. “If you want me to beg like Mussolini, I’m here. Look, Brandon. I’m sorry about telling you to kill yourself. And no, I’m doing this to get your love. I’m just picturing it in my head... just someone hanging like that. It sure is unpleasant.” “Didn’t think you’d get worse, adding ‘fascist’ to ‘sociopath’ on your resume or whatever.” “I’m picturing you, Brandon. I see your face in that husk of a dictator. Whatever he does, whatever evil... It’s gone. It’s time for the gods to judge him for it. But you, you’re still alive. Still breathing the air I give you— The air they gave us. This level.” “What are you even talking about?” I drew in closer and closer. Apologies never fix things. If it did, I’d rather have Hestia remain fragmented. “I hate myself, okay? Is that what you want to hear? That’s why I’m doing this.” “That’s one way to shill your humility.” “It’s not.” As if to move further away from me, she finally climbed up. Multiple, tiny black holes had sucked out any sense of fulfillment that wasn’t even there in the first place. Just its air that remained. “I’m going down, whether you like it or not. I know you want me to kill myself.” “No, Hestia. That’s not what I meant—” “Too late. Sorry.” “Wait, Hestia. You don’t deserve to die. I don’t either. Just let it go.” “You don’t need me anyway.” “I need you, all right? I need you. I’m a human being, and human beings don’t do this. You said that, right? You said we’re social creatures. We depend on one another.” “Yes. That’s why I’m going down there.” I could barely hear her sigh. She dropped a foot into the abyss. “If I live, you can follow. If I don’t, then so be it.” “There’s got to be another way. Hestia. Some kind of loophole—” “What option do we have? Starve out? Oh, right. That’s not even an option.” “Hestia, do you really believe we’ve been here for a while?” “I know you believe that.” “I hit my head, okay? I didn’t mean it.” “Brandon, I’m sorry. There’s no other way. Once you get to this point, there’s no U-turn. You’ve missed the exit, and to go back, you have to do it all over again. And who knows you’ll miss the chance when the time comes?”

Hestia had been falling for a thousand years in the dark void, waiting for something to catch her. One leg brushing against the corner of a stained carpet would’ve been enough for her to return. But that never happens. That never happens. The fall was deep, frozen in pure blackness, broken only by the occasional noise she sees when she closes her eyes. And then imagine those never-ending nightmares replaying again and again for eternity, for eternity squared, until kneeling becomes more comfortable than floating. Now, condense that into someone’s brain. Then imagine that someone blinking toward the fact, and see what they do for the rest of the day. Hestia was that someone. And she went on. Cooked her chicken on the hearth without a second thought. Willing to take the step forward and risk never tasting the ground, or anything on it, ever again until the second Big Bang. And then she dropped another leg in there. “You sound weirder than before. You’re loud when you’re whispering.” “Hestia, I know you think you’re funny, but that switch-up isn’t convincing me.” “Do I have to risk my soul for you?” “You don’t need to risk anything. This is your decision, and I’ve got nothing to do with it.” The splat of her foot against the ground rang through the edifice. She stood up. With only her hands keeping her from eternal damnation, she gripped the marble and stared across the empty void, trying to see if there was even a single photon in it. But the only light in that void was her own sclera—and it wasn’t even bright, just white. A contradiction in a sea of conformity. She should’ve gotten rid of herself right then. What’s her use? Head turned back, chest tight. “My use is to sacrifice for you,” she said. “What?” “You don’t even know how to keep your mind quiet.
